proposition_text: Domain-specific fine-tuning of a BERT-family transformer on labelled in-domain text outperforms zero-shot prompting of larger general-purpose LLMs on three-class sentiment classification of central-bank economic narratives.
theoretical_basis: Domain adaptation theory: in-domain pre-training shifts the representation distribution toward task-relevant features, and supervised fine-tuning with domain labels resolves the mapping from those features to class boundaries that zero-shot prompting only approximates from general priors.
scope_conditions: Tested on Beige Book chunks (256 tokens). Effect should generalize to other low-resource, mixed-tone economic communications, but the specific magnitude depends on availability of high-quality human labels and the gap between general pre-training corpora and the target domain.

proposition_text: General-purpose pre-trained LLMs systematically over-predict the mixed/neutral sentiment class when classifying multi-topic economic narratives, because zero-shot prompting biases the model toward hedging in the presence of any conflicting cues, regardless of which signals are dominant.
theoretical_basis: RLHF-trained LLMs are tuned toward calibrated, hedged responses; in the absence of domain priors about which topics carry more weight, they default to mixed when any divergent signals appear in a passage.
scope_conditions: Multi-topic narrative passages with co-occurring positive and negative signals. May not hold for short, single-topic texts where one sentiment dominates.

proposition_text: Sentiment extracted from Beige Book reports by a domain-fine-tuned classifier correlates with contemporaneous and short-horizon-future U.S. macroeconomic conditions (positively with GDP growth and CPI, negatively with unemployment), and contains incremental forecasting information beyond autoregressive lags of those indicators.
theoretical_basis: Beige Books aggregate qualitative business-contact reports across 12 Federal Reserve districts; this anecdotal signal contains real-economy information not yet reflected in published macro statistics, especially at 2-3 Beige-Book-period horizons.
scope_conditions: U.S. economy, 1970-2023 Beige Book corpus, eight publications per year. Strongest at 2-3 period horizons for GDP; weaker for variables (CPI, unemployment) whose own AR processes already explain >87% of variance.
